text: So this patient went for a stent and so the mild cough turned into a big cough and this is what we look on to see in post-operative day 27 with the bronchoscopy showing actually our esophageal stent.
Simon, I think you probably never see this, but what would you do?
Firstly, I share John's view just now, and on the previous picture, I was really worried.

So traditionally, what I tend to do is to do the triple tube procedure, what I call, I put in a nasal duodenal tube for feeding, I put in a nasal gastric tube for decompression, and sometimes I, through the hole, I feed a tube down for drainage.
This is combined with external drainage from the outside if my radiologist can put in a tube.
So I think drainage is the key and then wait for everything to settle.
I think that is the main approach that I would use.
Stents, sometimes I put in, but this is exactly the problem that I'm worried about.

He was a very sick gentleman to start off with because of peripheral vascular disease.
He had no toes, right below the amputation and a hemi foot amputation on the left side with clinical T2N0.
We redebrided it.
He wasn't sick at this time.
We redebrided it.
He did have some ischemia necrosis around three centimeters.
So luckily, we had enough conduit to debride.

Right main stem bronchus is here.
Because unfortunately, the omentum had completely became inflamed and was not usable as a flap itself.
The patient, surprisingly, did well.
This was around six years ago.
He still comes to my office with his motorized scooter.

And I've been burnt by this in the past.

Dr. No, certainly if there's a leak on day one or day two or some early leak that's technical or something, then I would revise, but not at day nine and not with that endoscopic view and the picture you've presented.
I think we would also consent the patient for an ease of stoma in the neck, but the last patients we did, we really tried to reanestomose if possible.
And actually we also did a patient where we put in prophylactically an endo-sponge and that really worked well also.

: My Wayne, how, how do the patients tolerate the tube in the nose for that length of time.
Is it a comfortable to Dr. I use.
I use the Blake drain, which is the soft elastic tube, similar to a soft elastic NG tube.
So I will admit that they're not frequently super happy with me about it, but I think that
that the alternative of taking their conduit out or re-operation, they'll handle it.
So I frequently do have to keep them in the hospital.
So we still need to work on some technology.
But as of now, it's an inpatient procedure and they have a blake in their nose.
And I sew it to their philtrum and they do fine with it.
Actually, the tube is not wider than a normal feeding tube and it goes out through the nose.
Of course, it's not really comfortable, but it's really very much okay and they can really deal with it.
And I agree with you, Wayne, that twice a week is not necessary, but we tend to do it really in the beginning to make the cavity really, really clean.
If it's really big and has a lot of purulent material, you want to have it clean.
And that's the only reason to do it more often.
And then we extend the time period to switch.
I just helped one of my colleagues over at Virginia go through a process where she had five centimeters of circumferential necrosis, and she did not want to reoperate.
And she went through three sponge changes, and they were able to save the whole conduit, and the patient's doing great with an intact conduit.

So, you know, if your anastomosis is typically down at the inferior pulmonary ligament, you know, where Lorenzo puts his, then I think that you can talk at all these things.
But we tend to put them a couple centimeters above the azagus arch, and it's going to be behind the airway.
And I've seen disasters and mortalities from stents eroding into airways in this situation.
So if you're going to try to save this conduit, and I say if, that's a decision that you have to make yourself with how healthy the patient is.
I would really emphasize that a sponge is the way to go here if you're going to try to save it.
If your stent is not going to be behind the airway, then I think you can try that.
And Lorenzo was able to do that in this situation and salvage a conduit on a patient who must have been otherwise pretty healthy.
